title: The Encoder-Decoder Split
concept: The encoder compresses the input into a single vector; the decoder takes this vector and generates the target sequence step-by-step.

exercises.prompt: What is the Encoder-Decoder split structure do modern translation setup do you think the 'split' the work style does the Encoder-Decoder resemble the 'Encoder-Decoder' models are made of:Decoder' models,What best describes the Encoder-Decoder split?, what is the Encoder-Decoder architecture?

options.text: A single network that does not share data

exercises.explain: The encoder and decoder are two specialized neural networks that work together to translate or transform sequences.

cards.title: Passing the Baton
cards.body:
How do AI models translate entire sentences from one language to another when the word order changes completely? 

They use a tag-team system where one half digests the input, and the other half builds the output!

cards.title: The Encoder-Decoder Split
cards.body:
An Encoder-Decoder model splits the work into two networks. 

The Encoder reads the entire input and compresses it into a single summary vector. 

The Decoder takes this vector and uses it to generate a brand-new output sequence, one word or token at a time.

cards.title: The Translator Analogy
cards.body:
Think of a translator at a global summit. 

First, they listen to an entire English speech and summarize the core meaning in their head (the Encoder). 

Then, using only that mental summary, they speak the translation in French sentence by sentence (the Decoder).

cards.title: Translation in Action
cards.body:
In Google Translate, when you type "How are you?", the encoder processes those three words and bundles their meaning into a numeric vector. 

The decoder takes that vector and outputs "Cómo", then "estás", and finally "?".

cards.title: Blind to the Input
cards.body:
Myth: The decoder looks at the original input sentence word-by-word as it translates.

Reality: The decoder never actually sees the original input words! It only looks at the compressed vector passed to it by the encoder.

cards.title: Comparing the Roles
cards.body: The split separates input processing from output generation.

left.text: Reads the input sentence and packs its meaning into a single vector.

right.text: Takes the vector and generates the output sequence step-by-step.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary role of the Encoder in this architecture?

options.text: It predicts the very last word of the output.
It compresses the input sequence into a single vector.
It directly prints the translated words.
It translates words one-by-one without looking at the whole sentence.

exercises.explain: The encoder's job is to read and compress the input sequence into a single vector of numbers that captures its meaning.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The decoder has to wait until the encoder finishes compressing the input before it can start generating the target sequence.
exercises.explain: Yes, the encoder must process and finish compressing the entire input sequence before the decoder can start generating the output.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to show who generates the final output.
exercises.sentence: While the encoder packages the input, the [blank] unpacks it to generate the translation.
exercises.explain: The encoder creates the summary vector from the input, and the decoder reads that vector to write the final translation.

exercises.prompt: Which of these systems is using an Encoder-Decoder approach?

options.text: System A: Translates word-for-word using a bilingual dictionary.
System B: Reads the whole English sentence, creates a concept vector, then generates French.
Neither system uses an encoder-decoder structure.

exercises.explain: System B represents a typical translation AI using an encoder-decoder setup, where the input is understood as a whole before generating the output. System A is just replacing words, which doesn't handle grammar changes.

exercises.prompt: Why can the single vector between the encoder and decoder become a 'bottleneck' for very long sentences?

options.text: The encoder refuses to process long sentences.
Fitting a massive input into a single vector can lose important details.
The decoder starts translating before the encoder is done.
The vector becomes too large for the computer's memory.

exercises.explain: The bottleneck is the compressed vector created by the encoder. If the input sequence is too long, forcing all that detail into one fixed-size vector can cause the model to lose important information before the decoder even starts.
